messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_ashrae2024.pdf
Equipment class: valve

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_20d3ad52d20b8811", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Control Valve Authority Range", "summary": "Valve authority should be 30-50% for piping systems with 2:1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios, with higher Δt coils requiring less authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "valve_authority", "title": "Control Valve Authority Range", "summary": "Valve authority should be 30-50% for piping systems with 2:1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios, with higher Δt coils requiring less authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided."}, "evidence": "The analysis should refer to thesystem selection matrix (such as in Table 1) and the reasons for scor -
ing.With each HV AC system considered, the design engineer should
note the criteria associated with each selection. Issues such as close-tolerance temperature and humidity control may eliminate someHV AC systems from consideration. System constraints, noted witheach analysis, may eliminate potential HV AC systems. Advantagesand disadvantages of each system should be noted with the scoringfrom the HV AC system selection matrix. This process shouldreduce HVAC selection to one or two optimum choices for pre -
sentation to the owner. Examples of similar installations for otherowners can be included with this report to support the final recom -
mendation. Identifying a third party for an endorsement allows theowner to inquire about the success of other HV AC installations.

ing.With each HV AC system considered, the design engineer should
note the criteria associated with each selection. Issues such as close-tolerance temperature and humidity control may eliminate someHV AC systems from consideration. System constraints, noted witheach analysis, may eliminate potential HV AC systems. Advantagesand disadvantages of each system should be noted with the scoringfrom the HV AC system selection matrix. This process shouldreduce HVAC selection to one or two optimum choices for pre -
sentation to the owner. Examples of similar installations for otherowners can be included with this report to support the final recom -
mendation. Identifying a third party for an endorsement allows theowner to inquire about the success of other HV AC installations.

• System integration with other building systems
• Other HV AC goals• Security and environmental health and safety criteria• Building envelope• Project timeline (design, equipment delivery, commissioning,
training, and construction)
• Basis of design• HV AC system analysis and selection matrix• System narratives• Budget costs (first cost, operating cost, and energy cost)• Final recommendation(s)
A brief outline of each of the final selections should be provided
along with overall budget and/or cost per square metre. In addition,
HV AC systems deemed inappropriate should be noted as havingbeen considered but not found applicable to meet the owner’s pri-mary HVAC goal.
The report should include an HV AC system selection matrix that
identifies the one or two suggested HV AC system selections (pri -
mary and secondary, when applicable), system constraints, and
other constraints and considerations.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_ee2fea0a0afab536", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Coil Air Face Velocity for Dehumidifying Coils", "summary": "Maximum coil air face velocity should be limited to prevent water carryover; dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "coil_face_velocity", "title": "Coil Air Face Velocity for Dehumidifying Coils", "summary": "Maximum coil air face velocity should be limited to prevent water carryover; dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s."}, "evidence": "For
information on calculating a dehumidi fying coil’s sensible heat ratio,
see the section on Performance of Dehumidifying Coils, or Appen-dix D of Cooling and Heating Load Calculation Principles  (Peder-
sen et al. 1998). For a given coil surface design and arrangement, therequired sensible heat ratio may be  satisfied by wide variations in
and combinations of air face velocity, in-tube temperature, flow rate,entering air temperature, coil depth, etc., although the variationsmay be self limiting. The maximum coil air face velocity should belimited to a value that prevents water carryover into the air ductwork.Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selectedin the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.
Operating ratings of dehumidifyi ng coils for factory-assembled,
self-contained air conditioners are generally determined in conjunc-
tion with laboratory testing for the system capacity of the complete
unit assembly.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_d9f400ddbe1ba54e", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Standard Rating Point for Unitary Equipment", "summary": "Standard rating point: 54 L/s per kW of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kW. For water coils, circuitry operates at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL/s per kW).", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "standard_rating_point", "title": "Standard Rating Point for Unitary Equipment", "summary": "Standard rating point: 54 L/s per kW of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kW. For water coils, circuitry operates at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL/s per kW)."}, "evidence": "1.4 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment  (SI)

ures 25 and 26). Typically, authorities in the range of 30 to 50% (for
piping systems with 2: 1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios) are
adequate for HV AC coil applications, with higher t coils requiring
less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided
because they lead to a flow characteristic shift that makes the equal-percentage valve appear to be more linear in nature (Figures 27 and
28). For the three-way valve shown in Figure 23, the full-open pres-
sure drop should be from C to D.  The pressure drop in the bypass
balancing valve in the three-way valv e circuit should be set to equal
that in the coil (load), Chapter 47 discusses valve authority in threeway modulating applications.
Control valves should be sized on the basis of the required valve
coefficient C
v for the required pressure drop and flow. For more
information, see the section on Control Valve Sizing under Auto-
matic Valves in Chapter 47.", "source_pages": [3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_696b47d6ea636765", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Hydrophilic Coating Pressure Drop Reduction", "summary": "Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "hydrophilic_coating_pressure_drop", "title": "Hydrophilic Coating Pressure Drop Reduction", "summary": "Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil."}, "evidence": "Therefore, final selection involves
economic balancing of the initial co st of the coil against the operat-
ing costs of the coil geometry co mbinations available to adequately
meet the performa nce requirements.
The aluminum fin surfaces of new dehumidifying coils tend to in-
hibit condensate sheeting action until they have aged for a year. Hy-
drophilic aluminum fin surface coatings reduce water droplet surface
tension, producing a more evenly dispersed wetted surface action at
initial start-up. Manufacturers have tried different methods of apply-
ing such coatings, including dippi ng the coil into a tank, coating the
fin stock material, or subjecting the material to a chemical etchingprocess. Tests have s hown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure
drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil..", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_6818c6ffe827661f", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Temperatures", "summary": "Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "low_temp_heating_supply_temp", "title": "Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Temperatures", "summary": "Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K."}, "evidence": "1.4 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment  (SI)

other constraints and considerations.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_09bed10d53508686", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Stratified System Supply Velocity", "summary": "Stratified room air distribution systems typically use supply outlets with very low discharge velocities (e.g., 0.2 to 0.36 m/s based on total face area).", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "stratified_supply_velocity", "title": "Stratified System Supply Velocity", "summary": "Stratified room air distribution systems typically use supply outlets with very low discharge velocities (e.g., 0.2 to 0.36 m/s based on total face area)."}, "evidence": "The air diffusion performance index (ADPI)
is a useful tool in predicting outlet performance for fully mixedsystems; see Chapter 58 of the 2023 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC
Applications  for more information.
2.2 FULLY STRATIFIED SYSTEMS
Stratified room air distribution systems, ty pically called thermal
displacement ventilation , generally rely on supply outlets with
very low discharge velocities (e.g ., 0.2 to 0.36 m/s based on total
face area) to produce minimal room air entrainment so that much ofthe temperature difference betwee n supply and the occupied zone’s
air is preserved. With outlets plac ed low, and when in cooling mode,
cool supply air accumulates near the floor. Heat sources in the occu-
pied zone create convection pl umes that originate around the
boundaries of the heat source and rise naturally be cause of their
buoyancy.", "source_pages": [4], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_444f376be34a264d", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Air Diffusion Performance Index (ADPI) Tool", "summary": "The air diffusion performance index (ADPI) is a useful tool in predicting outlet performance for fully mixed systems.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "adpi_tool", "title": "Air Diffusion Performance Index (ADPI) Tool", "summary": "The air diffusion performance index (ADPI) is a useful tool in predicting outlet performance for fully mixed systems."}, "evidence": "The air diffusion performance index (ADPI)
is a useful tool in predicting outlet performance for fully mixedsystems; see Chapter 58 of the 2023 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC
Applications  for more information.
2.2 FULLY STRATIFIED SYSTEMS
Stratified room air distribution systems, ty pically called thermal
displacement ventilation , generally rely on supply outlets with
very low discharge velocities (e.g ., 0.2 to 0.36 m/s based on total
face area) to produce minimal room air entrainment so that much ofthe temperature difference betwee n supply and the occupied zone’s
air is preserved. With outlets plac ed low, and when in cooling mode,
cool supply air accumulates near the floor. Heat sources in the occu-
pied zone create convection pl umes that originate around the
boundaries of the heat source and rise naturally be cause of their
buoyancy.", "source_pages": [4], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_15ef3af6c4096e18", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Coil Freezing Threshold", "summary": "Freezing occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature falls below 0°C. It does not occur with standard coils unless refrigerant evaporating temperature at coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "coil_freezing_threshold", "title": "Coil Freezing Threshold", "summary": "Freezing occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature falls below 0°C. It does not occur with standard coils unless refrigerant evaporating temperature at coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated."}, "evidence": "In ho t, humid climates, the light-load
condition has a higher proportion of moisture and a correspond-
ingly lower proportion of sensible  heat. The result is higher dew
points in the conditioned spaces during light-load  conditions un-
less a special means for controlling inside dew points (e.g., reheator dual path) is used.
Fin surface freezing at light lo ads should be avoided. Freezing
occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature fallsbelow 0°C. Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort
installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the
coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C satu rated; the exact value depends on
the design of the coil, its opera ting dew point, and the amount of
loading. With coil and condensing units to balance at low tempera-
tures at peak loads (not a custom ary design choice), freezing may
occur when load suddenly decreases.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_3aa156ff7859abf1", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Ethylene Glycol Solution Rating Parameters", "summary": "For ethylene glycol solution: water velocity 0.3 to 1.8 m/s, entering dry-bulb –18 to 32°C, entering wet-bulb 15 to 27°C, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by mass.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "glycol_rating_parameters", "title": "Ethylene Glycol Solution Rating Parameters", "summary": "For ethylene glycol solution: water velocity 0.3 to 1.8 m/s, entering dry-bulb –18 to 32°C, entering wet-bulb 15 to 27°C, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by mass."}, "evidence": "1.4 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment  (SI)

identifies the one or two suggested HV AC system selections (pri -
mary and secondary, when applicable), system constraints, and
other constraints and considerations.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 5], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_75aeb8e1306132e4", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Airflow Ratings Standard Air Density", "summary": "Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "standard_air_density", "title": "Airflow Ratings Standard Air Density", "summary": "Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa."}, "evidence": "In completing this matrixassessment, the design engineer should identify the owner’s input tothe analysis. This input can also be applied as weighted multipliers,because not all criteria carry the same weighted value.
Many grading methods are available to complete an analytical
matrix analysis. Probably the simplest is to rate each item excellent,very good, good, fair, or poor. A numerical rating system such as 0to 10, with 10 equal to excellent and 0 equal to poor or not applica -
ble, can provide a quantitative result. The HV AC system with thehighest numerical value then becomes the recommended system toaccomplish the goal.
The system selection report should include a summary followed
by a more detailed account of the HV AC system analysis and systemselection. This summary should highlight key points and findingsthat led to the recommendation(s).", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}]}

Return JSON shape: {"verdicts":[{"candidate_id":"...","is_valid_hvac_knowledge":true,"reason":"short reason","category_if_not":null}]}
